worth_it_explanation: Lazy loading decides whether a long page requests every ad slot up front or holds off-screen slots until the reader scrolls toward them. Loading all slots at once on a page that few readers scroll through sends ad requests for impressions that are never seen, which wastes requests and depresses the viewability rate the buyers pay against. The check is a review of the GPT configuration a publisher already runs in their own page code, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete loading decision the publisher can test against revenue and viewability.
intro: GPT is the JavaScript library a publisher runs on their own pages to request and render ads from Google Ad Manager. On a long page, GPT can either request every ad slot the moment the page loads or hold the off-screen slots until the reader scrolls toward them. This subcategory is about that loading decision, whether lazy loading is configured and how its margins are tuned, rather than the slot definitions or the ad units themselves. These checks confirm that off-screen inventory is not requested all at once on pages readers rarely scroll through, and that the fetch and render margins load ads close to the viewport rather than too late or too early.

audit_checks.title: Long pages request all ad slots up front instead of lazy loading off-screen inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Requesting every slot on a long page at load time sends ad requests for off-screen impressions that many readers never scroll to, which inflates the request count and lowers the measured viewability rate. Because buyers pay against viewability, off-screen impressions that are requested but never seen depress the average price and waste the impression on inventory that could have stayed unsold until it mattered.

educate.base:
When a page loads, GPT by default requests every ad slot that has been defined and displayed, whether the slot sits at the top of the page or far below the fold. On a short page that is fine because the reader sees most of the slots. On a long page, an article that runs several screens or an infinite feed, the reader may never scroll past the first screen or two, so the slots near the bottom are requested but never seen.

An impression that is requested but never seen carries two costs. It adds an ad request that the publisher's ad server and the buyers process for nothing, and it counts against the viewability rate, the share of served impressions that were actually visible. Buyers pay against viewability, so a page that requests far more impressions than it shows reports a low viewability rate and can clear at a lower average price across the inventory.

Lazy loading changes the timing. Instead of requesting every slot up front, GPT holds the off-screen slots and requests each one only as the reader scrolls toward it. Google's own guidance frames this as the recommended approach for below-the-fold ads, deferring the fetch and render until the slots are close to entering the viewport so the browser does less work at load time and the requested impressions track the ones the reader actually reaches.

Lazy loading is configured in the page's GPT code rather than in the Google Ad Manager UI, so the audit reads the JavaScript on the page and the network requests it produces. A few signs point to off-screen slots being requested up front rather than lazy loaded:
  • The page code calls googletag.display() or refreshes every slot at load with no disableInitialLoad and no lazy-load configuration
  • The network panel shows ad requests for slots far below the fold firing immediately on page load before any scroll
  • The viewability rate in Google Ad Manager reporting sits well below the share of slots a reader typically reaches on a long page
You may want to confirm that long pages defer their off-screen ad requests rather than firing them all at load. One approach is to enable lazy loading for below-the-fold slots and compare requests, viewability, and revenue against the current up-front behavior.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, long-form articles and infinite-scroll feeds often place several ad slots down the page, and many readers leave before reaching the lower ones. You may want to lazy load the below-the-fold slots on those templates so the off-screen impressions are requested only as readers scroll toward them, which keeps the viewability rate honest on the inventory that buyers actually pay against.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, long category listings and product pages with editorial sections below the fold can carry ad slots that load before a shopper scrolls to them. You may want to lazy load the lower slots on those templates so the requests track the inventory shoppers reach, rather than firing every slot the moment the page opens.

investigate.intro: Review the page's GPT code and its network requests to see whether off-screen slots are requested at load time or held until the reader scrolls toward them.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open a long page on the site, open the browser's DevTools, and select the 'Network' tab.
Filter the requests for 'securepubads.g.doubleclick.net' (the GPT, or GPT, ad request endpoint) and reload the page without scrolling.
Count how many ad requests fire before any scroll, then scroll down and watch whether new requests fire as lower slots approach the viewport.
If every slot's request fires at load before any scroll, the page requests all slots up front. If requests fire as you scroll toward each slot, lazy loading is active.

gpt_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open the page and launch the Google Publisher Console by appending '?googfc' to the URL or using the googfc bookmarklet.
Open the 'Page request' or slot view and note which slots have requested ads and when, relative to their position on the page.

Read the page's GPT code in DevTools 'Sources' and look for a setConfig lazyLoad block, an enableLazyLoad() call, or disableInitialLoad paired with scroll-triggered refresh, which indicate lazy loading is configured.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a representative long page, whether off-screen ad slots are requested at load time or deferred until the reader scrolls toward them, and notes the viewability rate the page reports in Google Ad Manager. A long page that lazy loads its below-the-fold slots, so requests track the inventory readers reach, passes. A long page that requests every slot up front, with off-screen impressions firing before any scroll and a viewability rate well below the share of slots a reader typically reaches, is a fail recorded with the page templates affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether off-screen slots are requested at load or deferred, and the page's viewability rate, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that long pages on {{site_name}} request every ad slot at load time rather than deferring the off-screen slots until a reader scrolls toward them. Requesting slots a reader may never reach adds ad requests for impressions that are never seen and lowers the viewability rate that buyers pay against, which can clear the inventory at a lower average price. {{voice_recommend}} enabling lazy loading for the below-the-fold slots on the long-page templates through GPT, so each off-screen slot is requested only as it approaches the viewport. After the change, compare ad requests, viewability, and revenue against the current up-front behavior to confirm the deferred loading holds or improves revenue while raising viewability.

audit_checks.title: Lazy-load fetch and render margins are left at defaults rather than tuned to the page
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A fetch or render margin set too tight requests the ad only as the slot enters the viewport, so the reader sees a blank slot while the ad loads. A margin set too wide requests the ad so far ahead that it behaves much like up-front loading, which gives back the viewability and request savings lazy loading was meant to produce. Margins left at a single default across every template rarely fit the scroll speed and slot spacing of each page.

educate.base:
Lazy loading in GPT is controlled by two margins. The fetch margin decides how far ahead of the viewport an off-screen slot requests its ad, and the render margin decides how far ahead the ad is drawn into the slot. GPT expresses both as a percentage of viewport height through the lazyLoad configuration, with a fetchMarginPercent and a renderMarginPercent, and a mobileScaling value that scales the margins on mobile devices where scroll speed and screen size differ from desktop.

The margins decide whether lazy loading actually helps. Set the fetch margin too tight, close to zero, and the ad is requested only as the slot reaches the viewport, so the reader scrolls onto a blank slot and waits while the ad loads, which hurts the reading experience and can still miss the viewability window. Set the margins too wide, several screens ahead, and the slot requests its ad so early that the behavior approaches up-front loading, which gives back the request and viewability savings lazy loading was meant to produce.

The right margin depends on the page. A fast-scrolling feed needs a wider fetch margin so the ad is ready before the reader arrives, while a slow-read article can use a tighter margin without showing blanks. A single default margin applied across every template treats pages that behave differently as if they were the same, so the audit question is whether the margins were tuned to the page or left at a default value that was never revisited.

The margins live in the page's GPT configuration, so the audit reads the JavaScript and watches how slots load while scrolling. A few signs point to margins left at defaults rather than tuned:
  • The lazyLoad configuration uses one fetchMarginPercent and renderMarginPercent across every template regardless of how each page scrolls
  • Readers scroll onto blank ad slots that fill in after a visible delay, which points to a fetch margin set too tight
  • Network requests for lower slots fire several screens before the reader reaches them, which points to a margin set so wide it approaches up-front loading
You may want to confirm that the fetch and render margins fit how each template scrolls rather than sharing one default. One approach is to watch slots load while scrolling at a normal reading pace, then adjust the margins per template so ads are ready just before they enter the viewport.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a long-read article and a fast-scrolling infinite feed call for different margins because the feed reader moves past slots quickly while the article reader lingers. You may want to set a wider fetch margin on the feed templates so ads are ready before the reader arrives, and a tighter margin on the article templates so off-screen slots are not requested far ahead of when they are seen.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a long category grid that shoppers scan quickly behaves differently from a product page they read slowly. You may want to tune the fetch and render margins per template, with a wider margin where shoppers scroll fast and a tighter one where they linger, so ads load just before each slot enters the viewport rather than blank slots appearing or requests firing too early.

investigate.intro: Review the lazy-load margins in the page's GPT configuration and watch how slots load while scrolling, to see whether the margins fit the page or sit at an untuned default.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open the page and open DevTools 'Sources', then find the GPT configuration and read the lazyLoad block for fetchMarginPercent, renderMarginPercent, and mobileScaling.
Record the margin values and whether the same values are used across different page templates.
Switch to the 'Network' tab, filter for 'securepubads.g.doubleclick.net', and scroll the page at a normal reading pace.
Watch how far ahead of each slot its request fires. A request firing several screens early points to a wide margin, and a slot that appears blank before it fills points to a margin set too tight.

gpt_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open the page and launch the Google Publisher Console by appending '?googfc' to the URL or using the googfc bookmarklet.
Scroll the page and watch the slot states change from defined to requested to rendered, noting how early each off-screen slot requests relative to entering the viewport.
Record whether ads are ready just before their slots are seen, appear blank for a moment, or load far ahead of when the reader reaches them.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the fetch and render margin values from the lazyLoad configuration, whether they vary by template, and how slots load while scrolling at a normal pace. Margins tuned so ads are ready just before their slots enter the viewport, adjusted where templates scroll at different speeds, pass. Margins left at a single default that produces blank slots on entry or requests fired several screens early are a fail recorded with the templates where the timing does not fit.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the fetch and render margin values and how slots load while scrolling above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the lazy-load fetch and render margins on {{site_name}} sit at a single default rather than being tuned to how each template scrolls. A margin set too tight loads ads as the slot reaches the viewport, so readers see blank slots, while a margin set too wide requests ads several screens early and gives back the savings lazy loading was meant to produce. {{voice_recommend}} watching how slots load while scrolling each template at a normal pace, then adjusting the fetchMarginPercent and renderMarginPercent in the GPT lazyLoad configuration per template so ads are ready just before they enter the viewport. After the change, recheck the scroll behavior and the viewability rate to confirm the margins fit each page.
